title: The Silmarillion
author: J. R. R. Tolkien
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "The Silmarillion" book by J. R. R. Tolkien?
Options:
A. As a defiance to Melkor, Varda hung the crown of seven bright stars called Valacirca, the Sickle of the Valar and an omen of destiny, high up in the northern sky. It's said that just when Varda finished her extensive work, as Menelmacar first walked the sky and the blue fire of Helluin glimmered in the mists at the edges of the world, right then the Firstborn Children of Ilúvatar, the Elves, awoke. Next to the starry pool of Cuiviénen, the Water of Awakening, they rose from their slumber by Ilúvatar and while they stayed quietly by Cuiviénen, their eyes saw the stars in the sky for the first time.
B. As a test to Melkor, Varda put the crown of seven bright stars called Valacirca, the Sickle of the Valar and a sign of destiny, high in the north. It is said that just as Varda finished her long work, when Menelmacar first walked the sky and the blue fire of Helluin glimmered in the mists at the edges of the world, at that time the Firstborn of Ilúvatar, the Elves, woke up. By the starry waters of Cuiviénen, the Water of Awakening, they rose from their sleep by Ilúvatar. As they lived quietly by Cuiviénen, their eyes first saw the stars in the sky.
C. And high in the north as a challenge to Melkor she set the crown of seven mighty stars to swing, Valacirca, the Sickle of the Valar and sign of doom. It is told that even as Varda ended her labours, and they were long, when first Menelmacar strode up the sky and the blue fire of Helluin flickered in the mists above the borders of the world, in that hour the Children of the Earth awoke, the Firstborn of Ilvatar. By the starlit mere of Cuivinen, Water of Awakening, they rose from the sleep of Ilvatar; and while they dwelt yet silent by Cuivinen their eyes beheld first of all things the stars of heaven.
D. Varda created a crown of seven stars called Valacirca, the Sickle of the Valar, as a challenge to Melkor and a sign of fate, placing it high in the northern sky. The story goes that just as Varda ended her long labors, when Menelmacar first strode across the sky and the blue fire of Helluin flickered in the mists at the borders of the world, at that moment the Firstborn Children of Ilúvatar, the Elves, awoke. Beside the starry waters of Cuiviénen, the Water of Awakening, they rose from the sleep of Ilúvatar and as they lingered silently by Cuiviénen, their eyes beheld the stars in the heavens for the first time.
Answer:

options.A: As a defiance to Melkor, Varda hung the crown of seven bright stars called Valacirca, the Sickle of the Valar and an omen of destiny, high up in the northern sky. It's said that just when Varda finished her extensive work, as Menelmacar first walked the sky and the blue fire of Helluin glimmered in the mists at the edges of the world, right then the Firstborn Children of Ilúvatar, the Elves, awoke. Next to the starry pool of Cuiviénen, the Water of Awakening, they rose from their slumber by Ilúvatar and while they stayed quietly by Cuiviénen, their eyes saw the stars in the sky for the first time.
options.B: As a test to Melkor, Varda put the crown of seven bright stars called Valacirca, the Sickle of the Valar and a sign of destiny, high in the north. It is said that just as Varda finished her long work, when Menelmacar first walked the sky and the blue fire of Helluin glimmered in the mists at the edges of the world, at that time the Firstborn of Ilúvatar, the Elves, woke up. By the starry waters of Cuiviénen, the Water of Awakening, they rose from their sleep by Ilúvatar. As they lived quietly by Cuiviénen, their eyes first saw the stars in the sky.

It is told that even as Varda ended her labours, and they were long, when first Menelmacar strode up the sky and the blue fire of Helluin flickered in the mists above the borders of the world, in that hour the Children of the Earth awoke, the Firstborn of Ilvatar. By the starlit mere of Cuivinen, Water of Awakening, they rose from the sleep of Ilvatar; and while they dwelt yet silent by Cuivinen their eyes beheld first of all things the stars of heaven.
options.D: Varda created a crown of seven stars called Valacirca, the Sickle of the Valar, as a challenge to Melkor and a sign of fate, placing it high in the northern sky. The story goes that just as Varda ended her long labors, when Menelmacar first strode across the sky and the blue fire of Helluin flickered in the mists at the borders of the world, at that moment the Firstborn Children of Ilúvatar, the Elves, awoke. Beside the starry waters of Cuiviénen, the Water of Awakening, they rose from the sleep of Ilúvatar and as they lingered silently by Cuiviénen, their eyes beheld the stars in the heavens for the first time.
